notes.anchor: the consuls' provinces had been allotted them by your common consent
notes.note: By the Senate's vote the consuls of 57 (Lentulus and Metellus) were assigned their post-consular provinces in advance; Cicero's friends backed this to win the consuls over, but it stripped the friendly tribunes of a bargaining lever.

notes.anchor: they have lost all the force of their own right
notes.note: The tribunes had hoped to control the allotment of the consular provinces and thereby bind the consuls to Cicero's recall; the Senate's pre-emptive grant gave that prize away for nothing.

notes.anchor: got the same thing through the people
notes.note: A measure could be carried either by senatorial decree or by a vote of the popular assembly; Cicero notes the consuls could not have forced it through the assembly over a tribune's veto.

notes.anchor: the Senate would decree nothing before our case had been moved
notes.note: Cicero's partisans had hoped the Senate would transact no other business until his recall was settled; granting the provinces broke that resolve and so weakened the leverage of his cause.

notes.anchor: consuls-elect had their provinces allotted them ahead of time
notes.note: Provinces were normally assigned to sitting consuls, not to consuls-elect; Cicero calls the early allotment unprecedented and a worrying precedent for hasty senatorial decrees.

notes.anchor: Metellus, who was setting aside his quarrel with me
notes.note: Q. Caecilius Metellus Nepos, consul of 57 and an old adversary of Cicero from 62 BC, was now publicly burying that feud to let the recall proceed.

notes.anchor: the fourth day before the Ides of December
notes.note: The Ides fell on the 13th in December; reckoned inclusively, this dates the letter to 10 December 58 BC.
